name: Greedy Reachable Frontier
summary: Scan left to right tracking the farthest index reachable so far. If the cursor ever moves past that frontier, the last index is unreachable; otherwise it is reachable.
visualizationIntent: init (L3-4): the nums array is shown; farthest starts at 0, so only index 0 is inside the reachable frontier. loop (L5): the cursor i advances one cell to the right (current). check (L6): test whether i is still within the frontier (idx <= farthest). return-false (L7): i has escaped past farthest — the cursor cell flashes error and we return false (unreachable). update (L8-9): reach = i + nums[i]; farthest grows to max(farthest, reach), extending the reachable frontier rightward (cells 0..farthest take the frontier state). return-true (L10): the cursor walked the whole array without ever escaping the frontier, so the last index is reachable — all cells settle as result.

lineExplanations.3: Track the farthest index reachable so far; you always start able to stand on index 0.
lineExplanations.4: Store the array length so the scan covers every index.
lineExplanations.5: Walk the cursor i across every index from left to right.
lineExplanations.6: Check whether index i has fallen beyond the farthest index we can reach.
lineExplanations.7: The cursor has outrun the reachable frontier, so the last index can never be reached — return false.
lineExplanations.8: From index i you can jump up to nums[i] steps, landing as far as reach = i + nums[i].
lineExplanations.9: Extend the frontier to whichever is farther: the old frontier or this new reach.
lineExplanations.10: The cursor crossed the whole array without ever escaping the frontier — the last index is reachable, return true.

syntaxExplanations.1: class Solution: — the class wrapper the platform expects your method to live inside.
syntaxExplanations.2: def canJump(self, nums): — nums holds the maximum jump length available at each index.
syntaxExplanations.3: farthest = 0 — the greatest index reachable so far; you begin standing on index 0.
syntaxExplanations.4: n = len(nums) — the number of indices to scan.
syntaxExplanations.5: for i in range(n): — visit each index i from 0 to n-1.
syntaxExplanations.6: if i > farthest: — has the cursor moved past every index we could actually reach?
syntaxExplanations.7: return False — index i is unreachable, so the last index is too.
syntaxExplanations.8: reach = i + nums[i] — the farthest index you can land on by jumping from i.
syntaxExplanations.9: farthest = max(farthest, reach) — keep the larger of the old frontier and the new reach.
syntaxExplanations.10: return True — no index was ever out of reach, so the last index is reachable.
